question: How does Express route a request through its middleware stack?

shellCommands: "C:\\Windows\\System32\\WindowsPowerShell\\v1.0\\powershell.exe" -Command "rg -n \"Route\\.prototype\\.dispatch = function dispatch|Route\\.prototype\\.all = function all|Route\\.prototype\\[method\\] = function \\(handler\\)\" node_modules\\router\\lib\\route.js"

"C:\\Windows\\System32\\WindowsPowerShell\\v1.0\\powershell.exe" -Command "rg -n \"app\\.handle = function handle|this\\.router\\.handle\\(req, res, done\\)|app\\.use = function use|app\\.route = function route\" lib\\application.js"

"C:\\Windows\\System32\\WindowsPowerShell\\v1.0\\powershell.exe" -Command "rg -n \"Router\\.prototype\\.handle = function handle|Router\\.prototype\\.use = function use|Router\\.prototype\\.route = function route|processParams\\(|trimPrefix\\(|next\\('route'\\)|next\\('router'\\)\" node_modules\\router\\index.js"
baseline.answer:
Express routes a request by walking a linear stack of `Layer`s and invoking the first matching middleware or route handler, then continuing only when that handler calls `next()`.

- `app.handle()` prepares the request/response, sets the prototypes, and hands off to the app router. See [`lib/application.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/lib/application.js#L152) and [`lib/application.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/lib/application.js#L177).
- The router keeps an ordered `stack` of layers built by `use()` and `route()/METHOD()`. See [`node_modules/router/index.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/index.js#L149) and [`node_modules/router/index.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/index.js#L362).
- On each `next()`, `Router#handle()` scans forward through the stack until a layer matches the current pathname. For route layers, it also checks the HTTP method. See [`node_modules/router/index.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/index.js#L149).
- If the layer is plain middleware from `use()`, Express trims the mounted path from `req.url`, updates `req.baseUrl`, and calls the middleware. That is how mounted routers and sub-apps work. See [`node_modules/router/index.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/index.js#L297).
- If the layer is a route, Express runs `Route#dispatch()`, which walks the route’s own handler stack in order. See [`node_modules/router/lib/route.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/lib/route.js#L98).
- `next('route')` skips the rest of the current route’s handlers. `next('router')` exits the current router entirely. See [`node_modules/router/lib/route.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/lib/route.js#L98) and [`node_modules/router/index.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/index.js#L149).
- If a handler throws or calls `next(err)`, Express switches to error flow and only calls 4-arg handlers until the error is handled. See [`node_modules/router/lib/layer.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/lib/layer.js#L106) and [`node_modules/router/lib/layer.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/lib/layer.js#L142).
- Route parameters are processed before the handler runs, via `router.param()` callbacks. See [`node_modules/router/index.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/index.js#L291) and the `processParams()` implementation lower in the same file.
- `HEAD` falls back to `GET` if no explicit `HEAD` handler exists, and `OPTIONS` can auto-generate an `Allow` response. See [`node_modules/router/lib/route.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/lib/route.js#L98) and [`node_modules/router/index.js`](C:/Users/samch/AppData/Local/Temp/samchon-graph-corpus/express/node_modules/router/index.js#L149).

In short: Express does not build a separate routing tree. It does an ordered, path-and-method match over middleware layers, rewrites `req.url` when entering mounted sub-routers, and advances only when handlers explicitly call `next()`.
